explanation: This LTL property captures the requirement that whenever the pilot is in control (standby) and the autopilot is in the NOMINAL state, then in the next state the autopilot will be in the STANDBY state. This ensures the autopilot transitions to STANDBY when the pilot is in control.

The LTL property states that whenever the pilot is in control (standby) and the autopilot is in the NOMINAL state, then in the next state, the autopilot must be in the STANDBY state. This captures the requirement that the autopilot should transition from NOMINAL to STANDBY when the pilot takes control.

This LTL property states that whenever the pilot is in control (standby is true), the autopilot will eventually transition to the STANDBY state. The property uses the G operator to indicate that this behavior should hold globally (at all times) and the F operator to indicate that the autopilot will eventually reach the STANDBY state.

The LTL property expresses that whenever the pilot is in control (standby is true), the autopilot will eventually transition from the NOMINAL state to the STANDBY state. This is achieved using the "G" (always) operator to ensure that the implication holds in every state and the "F" (eventually) operator to capture the transition to the STANDBY state. The boolean expression bool_exp3 represents the condition when the autopilot is in the NOMINAL state and the pilot is in control. The boolean expression bool_exp2 represents the condition when the autopilot is in the STANDBY state. This property ensures that the autopilot will switch to the STANDBY mode when the pilot takes control. It doesn't specify how long it takes to make this transition.

The LTL property ensures that whenever the pilot is in control (standby) and the autopilot is in the NOMINAL state, it will transition to the STANDBY state in the next time step. This captures the requirement that the autopilot should change states from NOMINAL to STANDBY when the pilot is in control.

The LTL property states that whenever the pilot is in control (standby), then in the next state the autopilot will transition to the STANDBY state. This captures the natural language by expressing the requirement that the autopilot should change states from NOMINAL to STANDBY when the pilot is in control (standby).

The LTL property captures the requirement that whenever the pilot is in control (standby), the autopilot will eventually transition from the NOMINAL state to the STANDBY state within a fixed duration of time.

This LTL property states that whenever the pilot is in control (standby), the autopilot will eventually transition to the STANDBY state from the NOMINAL state. The property uses the "G" operator to indicate that this condition should hold globally (for all time). The "->" operator is used to express the implication relationship between the pilot being in control and the autopilot transitioning to the STANDBY state. The "F" operator specifies that the event of the autopilot being in the STANDBY state will eventually occur. The chosen template ID is 19 as it allows us to express the implication between the pilot being in control (standby) and the eventual transition to the STANDBY state. It uses the "G" operator to ensure this holds globally for all time.

This LTL formula captures the requirement that whenever the autopilot is in the NOMINAL state and the pilot is in control (standby), then in the next state the autopilot will transition to STANDBY. It uses a G operator to ensure this holds globally across all states, an implication to connect the condition (NOMINAL state and pilot in control) to the consequence (transition to STANDBY), and an X operator to express the next state transition.

This LTL property captures the requirement that whenever the autopilot is in the NOMINAL state and the pilot is in control (standby), then in the next state the autopilot will transition to the STANDBY state. This ensures that the autopilot correctly responds to the pilot taking control.

This LTL property captures the requirement that whenever the pilot is in control (standby) and the autopilot is in the NOMINAL state, in the next state, the autopilot should transition to the STANDBY state. It uses the 'G' operator to ensure that this implication holds globally, meaning in all states. The boolean expression 'standby' represents the pilot being in control, and 'state_is_NOMINAL' represents the autopilot being in the NOMINAL state. The expression 'state_is_STANDBY' represents the autopilot being in the STANDBY state. The 'X' operator indicates the next state.

This LTL property states that whenever the pilot is in control (standby is true) and the autopilot is in the NOMINAL state, in the next state the autopilot will be in the STANDBY state. It captures the input natural language by stating the condition (pilot in control, autopilot in NOMINAL state) and the consequence (autopilot transitioning to STANDBY state).

The autopilot shall change states from NOMINAL to STANDBY when the pilot is in control of the vehicle (standby). The autopilot shall change states from NOMINAL to STANDBY, when standby is true, this LTL property states that whenever standby becomes true, the autopilot will transition into the STANDBY state in the next time step.

This LTL property ensures that whenever the pilot is in control (standby is true), the autopilot will eventually transition from NOMINAL to STANDBY. The chosen template captures this by stating that globally, if standby is true, then the autopilot will eventually transition to STANDBY (which implies being in STANDBY).

This LTL property ensures that whenever the pilot is in control (standby) and the autopilot is in the NOMINAL state, it will transition to the STANDBY state in the next time step.  The boolean expression "(state_is_NOMINAL & standby)" ensures that the autopilot is in NOMINAL mode and the pilot is in control. The boolean expression "state_is_STANDBY" checks if the autopilot transitioned to the STANDBY state. This property captures the intended behavior of the autopilot system, ensuring a smooth transition between states when the pilot takes control.

The LTL property captures the requirement that whenever the pilot is in control (standby) and the autopilot is in the NOMINAL state, it will transition to the STANDBY state in the next state. The chosen LTL template is a combination of implication and next operator, where the antecedent is the conjunction of standby and state_is_NOMINAL, and the consequent is the next of state_is_STANDBY.  This ensures that the autopilot will change to the STANDBY state immediately after the pilot takes control.

This LTL property captures the requirement that whenever the pilot is in control (standby is True), the autopilot will eventually transition to the STANDBY state from the NOMINAL state. The chosen template is a "G (bool_exp2 -> (X bool_exp3))" template, which means "Globally, if bool_exp2 is True, then in the next state, bool_exp3 will be True". In this case, bool_exp2 is the condition where the pilot is in control, and bool_exp3 is the autopilot transitioning to the STANDBY state from the NOMINAL state.

The LTL property captures the requirement that whenever the pilot is in control (standby is true) and the autopilot is in NOMINAL state, it should eventually transition to STANDBY state. This is achieved by using the "G" (always) operator to ensure that the implication holds true for all states. The implication itself states that if the pilot is in control (standby is true) and the autopilot is in the NOMINAL state (state_is_NOMINAL is true), then eventually the autopilot will be in the STANDBY state (state_is_STANDBY is true). This is represented by the "F" (eventually) operator and the atomic proposition "state_is_STANDBY".
